title: Why Investing Matters
body:
The single most powerful force in investing is compound returns. When your investment generates a return, and that return is reinvested, it begins generating its own returns. Over time, this creates exponential growth that is genuinely counterintuitive to the human brain. Consider: $10,000 invested at 8% annual return grows to $21,589 after ten years, $46,610 after twenty years, and $100,627 after thirty years. The investor contributed $10,000 exactly once. The remaining $90,627 was generated entirely by compounding. This is not a trick or an edge case — it is the mathematical reality that underpins virtually all long-term wealth creation.

The implications of compounding for wealth building are profound and should shape how you think about investing from your very first dollar. Starting early matters enormously. An investor who puts $5,000 per year into a diversified portfolio from age 25 to 35 — ten years of contributions totalling $50,000 — and then stops contributing entirely will typically have more wealth at age 65 than someone who starts contributing the same $5,000 per year at age 35 and continues for thirty years straight, contributing $150,000 total. The first investor contributed less than a third of the capital but ends up with more because their money had an extra decade of compounding. Every year you delay investing costs you more than you might imagine.

Beyond personal wealth, investing plays a critical role in how societies allocate resources. Capital markets are, in effect, a distributed decision-making system. When millions of investors independently assess opportunities and deploy their capital, the aggregate result — reflected in stock prices, bond yields, currency values, and property prices — represents the market's collective judgment about where resources should flow. Companies that create value attract investment and grow. Companies that destroy value see their access to capital shrink. This process is imperfect and prone to bubbles, manias, and mispricing, but over long periods it has proven remarkably effective at channelling resources toward productive uses. The countries with the deepest, most liquid capital markets have consistently been among the most innovative and economically dynamic.

Financial independence — the state where your investment income covers your living expenses — is achievable for far more people than typically realise it, but it requires understanding the mechanics of investing. The 4% rule, derived from historical market return data, suggests that a retiree can withdraw 4% of their portfolio value each year with a high probability of not running out of money over a 30-year retirement. This means a portfolio of $1 million can support $40,000 per year in withdrawals. Working backwards, the question becomes: how much do you need to invest, at what return, over what time period, to reach your target number? This is not abstract financial theory — it is the concrete arithmetic of personal freedom.

Economic literacy — understanding how interest rates, inflation, exchange rates, and fiscal policy affect asset prices and real purchasing power — is increasingly essential for navigating modern life. Inflation silently erodes the value of cash savings. A dollar today buys less than a dollar five years ago. Keeping your wealth in a savings account earning 1% while inflation runs at 3% means you are losing 2% of your purchasing power every year. Investing in assets that grow faster than inflation — equities, property, and certain alternatives — is not about getting rich. It is about not getting poorer.

The democratisation of investing through low-cost index funds and commission-free trading platforms means that the tools for building wealth are now accessible to almost everyone in developed economies. You do not need to pick stocks, time markets, or hire expensive advisors. A simple portfolio of diversified index funds, held consistently over decades with regular contributions, has historically outperformed the vast majority of actively managed funds. The barrier to building long-term wealth is not access or sophistication — it is knowledge, discipline, and the willingness to start.
keyPoints: Compound returns are the primary engine of long-term wealth creation — small amounts invested early grow exponentially over decades
Starting to invest early is dramatically more impactful than investing larger amounts later — time in the market beats timing the market
Capital markets serve a critical societal function by directing resources toward their most productive uses through collective price discovery
Financial independence is achievable through disciplined, long-term investing — the arithmetic of compounding makes it possible for ordinary earners
Inflation silently destroys the purchasing power of uninvested cash — investing is not just about growth, it is about preservation of real wealth
The shift from employer pensions to self-directed retirement accounts makes investment literacy a necessity, not a luxury
Low-cost index funds have made effective investing accessible to almost everyone — the primary barriers are now knowledge and discipline, not access or capital
Understanding investing provides a framework for understanding macroeconomics, geopolitics, and the forces that shape the global economy
furtherReading: 'The Psychology of Money' by Morgan Housel — behaviour and mindset in investing, grounded in real stories and data
'Your Money or Your Life' by Vicki Robin — financial independence through intentional relationship with money and investing
'The Simple Path to Wealth' by JL Collins — straightforward guide to building wealth through low-cost index fund investing
'Thinking, Fast and Slow' by Daniel Kahneman — the cognitive biases that affect investment decisions and how to recognise them
